skill.name: configuring-better-auth
skill.description: Guides Better Auth OAuth 2.1 and OIDC setup for auth servers and SSO clients, including PKCE, JWKS verification, token refresh, client registration, and logout patterns.
skill.summary: Configure Better Auth OAuth, OIDC, PKCE, JWKS, and SSO flows for Next.js applications.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: The static scanner reported many command, network, environment, and sensitive-data patterns, but most are documentation examples for OAuth setup and not evidence of malicious behavior. No prompt injection attempt was found. However, the references include insecure production guidance for browser token storage and JWKS private key encryption, so this community skill should not publish until corrected.

high_findings.title: Insecure Browser Token Storage Guidance
high_findings.description: The auth server reference recommends storing OAuth access and refresh tokens in localStorage. Tokens in localStorage are exposed to any successful XSS attack and conflict with safer httpOnly cookie guidance elsewhere in the skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly read and remove access_token and refresh_token from localStorage, then later state clients should only use localStorage. This is direct insecure token storage guidance for OAuth credentials.

high_findings.title: JWKS Private Key Encryption Disabled
high_findings.description: The JWKS configuration example disables private key encryption for simplicity. This weakens protection for signing keys and is unsafe guidance for production identity infrastructure.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The configuration directly sets disablePrivateKeyEncryption to true in a section that discusses production-scale JWKS. Legitimate local testing is possible, but the text presents it as general setup guidance.

medium_findings.title: JavaScript-Accessible PKCE Verifier Storage
medium_findings.description: The examples store PKCE verifiers in sessionStorage and plain document.cookie values that are readable by browser JavaScript. This can be acceptable for public clients, but it increases exposure if the app has XSS.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage pattern is clearly present and the values are browser-accessible. The risk is contextual because PKCE public clients often use browser storage, but stronger server-managed patterns are safer.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Server Trust Boundary
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add a third-party hosted MCP server for Better Auth guidance. This is not malicious by itself, but it adds an external source that can influence generated configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external MCP endpoint is explicit and central to the skill workflow. No credential exfiltration was found, but users must trust the remote MCP provider.

low_findings.title: Command and Network Static Hits Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command and fetch findings are npm install, curl, token exchange, JWKS, and logout examples for an OAuth guide. They should be reviewed before use, but they are not evidence of arbitrary command execution or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands are fenced documentation snippets with fixed package, curl, and setup commands. I did not find dynamic shell execution or hidden command invocation in the skill content.

low_findings.title: Filesystem, Weak Crypto, and Browser Credential Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported path traversal, weak crypto, and browser credential findings map to relative imports, SHA-256 or RS256 examples, and checklist text that warns against localStorage. No browser credential file access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are ordinary TypeScript imports, modern crypto algorithm labels, and a security checklist. I did not find code that reads browser credential files or uses MD5, SHA-1, or path traversal.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser localStorage Used for OAuth Tokens
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference stores and removes access_token and refresh_token values from localStorage, which is unsafe for bearer tokens in XSS-prone browser contexts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token names and localStorage calls are explicit in the reference implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: JWKS Private Key Encryption Disabled
dangerous_patterns.description: The JWT plugin example disables private key encryption, reducing defense in depth for identity-provider signing material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The setting is directly shown in the JWKS configuration block and is framed as a simplicity choice.

content.user_title: Configure Better Auth OAuth and SSO
content.value_statement: OAuth and OIDC setup is easy to misconfigure across auth servers and clients. This skill provides Better Auth patterns for PKCE, JWKS, token refresh, SSO, and logout workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides Better Auth OIDC provider configuration for centralized authentication servers.
Shows PKCE authorization and token exchange patterns for public SSO clients.
Provides JWKS verification examples using the jose library.
Covers OAuth client registration and first-party client seeding patterns.
Includes token refresh, logout, and protected route examples for Next.js.
Lists security checklist items for HTTPS, redirect URIs, CORS, and email verification.
limitations: Contains insecure token storage guidance that must be corrected before production use.
Focuses on Better Auth and Next.js, not every OAuth framework.
Does not deploy infrastructure or validate a live identity provider.
External MCP guidance depends on a third-party hosted server.

use_cases.title: Build a central auth server
use_cases.description: Create a Better Auth OIDC provider for multiple first-party applications.

use_cases.title: Add SSO to a Next.js app
use_cases.description: Connect a client application to an existing Better Auth server with PKCE.

use_cases.title: Review OAuth implementation details
use_cases.description: Check token expiry, JWKS, logout, and redirect URI handling before release.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Better Auth setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Better Auth OAuth and OIDC setup for two Next.js applications. Ask for missing architecture details first.

prompt_templates.title: Implement PKCE sign-in
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through implementing PKCE sign-in for a Next.js SSO client using Better Auth. Use httpOnly cookies for tokens.

prompt_templates.title: Configure JWKS verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to verify Better Auth ID tokens with JWKS in a Next.js route. Include issuer and audience checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit my auth flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Better Auth OAuth flow for token storage, redirect URI validation, PKCE handling, CORS, refresh, and logout risks.

output_examples.input: I need SSO between an auth server and two Next.js apps.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step architecture plan covering the Better Auth server, OIDC provider setup, public clients, redirect URIs, PKCE, JWKS, and logout behavior.

output_examples.input: My token exchange works, but protected routes fail after refresh.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist for cookie settings, refresh token handling, JWKS verification, token expiry, and redirect return paths.

output_examples.input: Review this Better Auth configuration before launch.
output_examples.output: A security-focused review highlighting token storage, CORS, redirect URI matching, email verification, client registration, and key management concerns.

best_practices: Use PKCE for public clients and avoid client secrets in browser code.
Store bearer tokens in httpOnly, Secure cookies when possible.
Verify issuer, audience, expiry, and signature on every protected route.
anti_patterns: Do not store OAuth access or refresh tokens in localStorage.
Do not disable signing key encryption for production identity systems.
Do not allow broad redirect URI patterns or untrusted origins.

faq.question: What does this skill help configure?
faq.answer: It helps configure Better Auth OAuth, OIDC, PKCE, JWKS verification, token refresh, and SSO client flows.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish now?
faq.answer: No. It contains insecure token storage and key management guidance that should be fixed first.

faq.question: Does it require an external service?
faq.answer: It references a hosted Better Auth MCP server and Better Auth documentation. Review that trust boundary before use.

faq.question: Can it generate a complete auth server?
faq.answer: It provides implementation patterns and checklists. You still need to adapt, test, and secure the final application.

faq.question: What should I change before production?
faq.answer: Remove localStorage token guidance, keep signing keys protected, enforce exact redirects, and test PKCE, JWKS, CORS, and logout.
